title: Torsion-induced gauge structure in curved quantum waveguides
authors: Xu-Yang Hou, Xianlong Gao, Hao Guo
abstract: We investigate the quantum dynamics of a particle confined to a space curve within the thin-layer quantization framework. For a nondegenerate scalar transverse mode, torsion does not enter the local effective Hamiltonian, which contains only the curvature-induced scalar geometric potential. In contrast, when a degenerate transverse subspace is retained, the rotation of the Frenet normal frame becomes dynamically relevant and generates a matrix-valued Abelian gauge potential. Using a projection-based derivation in a co-rotating Frenet-frame basis, we show that this effective gauge potential is directly determined by the local torsion of the curve. The resulting effective Hamiltonian takes a gauge-covariant form and produces two transverse-mode branches whose parabolic dispersions are shifted in opposite directions in momentum space. For closed curves, the associated holonomy is controlled by the integrated torsion and leads to geometric interference. These results provide a direct realization of a Wilczek--Zee-type connection induced purely by spatial geometry in curved quantum waveguides. We further construct a classical-wave analogue using the degenerate bending modes of an isotropic elastic rod, demonstrating that the same torsion-induced gauge structure appears in continuum wave physics.
summary: This theoretical work analyzes quantum particles confined to curved waveguides, demonstrating that the curve's torsion induces a gauge potential when the transverse motion is degenerate. This results in a measurable momentum splitting in the energy spectrum, an effect confirmed by a structural analogy with classical elastic rod bending.
main_problem: The paper investigates how the geometric property of torsion ($	au$) induces a gauge structure in the effective quantum Hamiltonian for a particle confined to a curved space curve.
main_result: Torsion generates a matrix-valued Abelian gauge potential ($A_s \propto 	au \sigma_y$) in the degenerate transverse subspace, leading to a momentum shift ($\pm 	au$) in the energy dispersion relation. This effect has a direct classical analogue in elastic rod bending.
method: The analysis uses thin-layer quantization and adiabatic approximation to derive the effective 1D Hamiltonian from the 3D Laplacian, comparing the scalar (non-degenerate) and degenerate transverse subspace cases.
model_or_system: The system is a particle confined to a space curve in $\mathbb{R}^3$, analyzed using the Frenet-Serret frame. The effective dynamics are governed by a gauge-covariant Hamiltonian when the transverse subspace is degenerate.
key_observables: Energy dispersion $E_{\pm}(k)$, group velocity $v_{\pm}(k)$, and the geometric phase/holonomy accumulated around closed loops.
important_parameters: Curvature ($\kappa$), Torsion ($	au$), and the transverse confinement length scale ($\ell_\perp$).
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on the thin-layer quantization limit and the adiabatic approximation, distinguishing between scalar and degenerate transverse mode subspaces.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the energy dispersion $E_{\pm}(k)$ and group velocity $v_{\pm}(k)$ for a helical wire, showing the splitting due to $	au$. The classical analogue is shown via the polarization evolution operator.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the general framework using thin-layer quantization, contrasting the scalar (no $	au$ dependence) and degenerate (gauge potential appears) cases. It then derives the explicit gauge potential and the resulting Hamiltonian, concluding with the comparison to classical wave physics (elastic rods) to confirm the structural isomorphism.
why_interesting: The emergence of a gauge field purely from the spatial geometry (torsion) is a fundamental concept in geometric physics, providing a concrete realization of a Wilczek-Zee type connection in condensed matter waveguides.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Helical curve with Frenet frames. The tangent T , principal normal N, and binormal B are shown at rep- resentative points along the curve. (b) Transverse mode dis- tributions of the twofold-degenerate ﬁrst excited states, with |1, 0⟩oriented along N and |0, 1⟩oriented along B. Solid and dashed contours indicate positive and negative amplitudes, respectively.
Figure 2: (a) Energy dispersion E±(k). (b) Group velocity v±(k). In both panels, the blue solid curves represent the + branch, whereas the red dashed curves represent the − branch.

Figure 3: (a) Closed helix with 16 turns. A particle travers- ing the loop acquires a geometric phase exp(iτLσy) due to the torsion-induced holonomy. (b) Interference intensity as a function of the geometric winding parameter ν = τL/(2π).
